decision_simulation: The real historical moment: 1871, Meiji-era Japan. The new government must decide how to design its first modern coinage — copy an existing trusted foreign standard (the Mexican silver dollar, dominant in Pacific trade) to gain instant international credibility, or create a wholly original Japanese design that asserted independence but risked rejection by foreign merchants and banks. The outcome was genuinely uncertain: Japan had only just ended over two centuries of relative isolation, and gaining global trust for a brand-new currency was far from guaranteed.Player choice: Match the Mexican 8-real's exact specifications, or design a fully independent Japanese standard.Historical resolution: Japan matched the Mexican coin's weight and diameter exactly for the 1870 1-yen coin, prioritizing immediate international trade credibility over design independence.

understand.economic_impact: By matching the Mexican 8-real's exact weight and diameter, Japan's first yen gained immediate credibility in Pacific trade networks already accustomed to Mexican silver dollars Smithsonian NMAH. Decades of near-zero Japanese interest rates in the modern era created the global "yen carry trade," where investors worldwide borrowed cheap yen to invest elsewhere [TH Script Writing Bible voice profile].
understand.political_impact: The New Currency Act of 1871 was only possible after the political centralization of haihan-chiken (the abolition of Japan's feudal domain system), directly linking currency unification to the broader Meiji-era dismantling of feudal power Meiji Jingu, Meiji Memorial Picture Gallery.
understand.why_it_still_matters: Imagine 270 different towns near you all printing their own separate money, and you had to figure out which was worth what every time you crossed a border — that was Japan before 1871. In one law, the government wiped that all away and created a single currency, deliberately built to match a foreign coin (from Mexico, on the other side of the Pacific) so the rest of the world would trust it right away. Aiko's story matters because it shows that sometimes the fastest way to build trust isn't to insist on doing things your own way — it's to prove you can meet the world's existing standard, perfectly, and then quietly become something entirely your own from there.
